question: Compare the SG design from Table 5.4-1 and Figure 5.4-1, explaining how the helical coil geometry achieves adequate heat transfer surface.

nuscale_ch05_0019.title: Reactor Coolant System Component and Subsystem Design
nuscale_ch05_0019.summary: This section describes the design of the NuScale Power Module’s Reactor Coolant System (RCS) components and subsystems, including the reactor pressure vessel, steam generators, safety valves, and associated piping and valves. It outlines the integration of these components within the RPV, highlights the boundaries between primary and secondary systems, and references other sections for detailed design bases of specific valves and internals.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the NuScale pressurizer, including the RPV upper shell, baffle plate, heater bundles, and associated components. It specifies the types of steel and nickel-based alloys used, their protective cladding, and compliance with relevant BPVC material specifications to ensure corrosion resistance and compatibility with operating environments.

nuscale_ch05_0020.title: Steam Generators
nuscale_ch05_0020.summary: Each NuScale Power Module (NPM) contains two steam generators (SGs) integrated within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which also houses other key components such as the pressurizer and safety valves. The steam generator system (SGS) includes feedwater and main steam piping, thermal relief valves, and access ports, with the RPV itself forming the outer pressure boundary for the SGs. The SG tubes and associated welds are part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, ensuring separation between the primary and secondary sides.

nuscale_ch05_0013.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0013.summary: This section lists references and standards used in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report regarding the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary. It includes guidelines and reports from the Electric Power Research Institute and the American Society of Mechanical Engineers, as well as tables summarizing relevant ASME code cases for materials and components used in nuclear facility applications.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and decay heat from the reactor core during non-LOCA events when normal cooling is unavailable, ensuring safe shutdown without external power. It maintains the reactor coolant system (RCS) temperature below 420°F within 36 hours, protecting fuel and pressure boundaries, and its reliability and risk significance are rigorously evaluated. The DHRS operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), with both systems able to function together if needed.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section describes the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS), which uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to ensure safe and efficient cooling, maintain water chemistry, and provide uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions, supporting both performance and safety requirements for each NuScale Power Module.

nuscale_ch05_0011.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Inservice Inspection and Testing
nuscale_ch05_0011.summary: This section describes the materials and standards used for threaded fasteners in key reactor coolant system components, emphasizing the use of Alloy 718 for its compatibility and stress corrosion cracking resistance. It also outlines that preservice and inservice inspections and testing of pressure-retaining components are conducted according to the latest applicable ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) requirements, as mandated by federal regulations, with inspection intervals and code editions specified in the inspection program.

nuscale_ch05_0023.title: Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents
nuscale_ch05_0023.summary: This section describes the testing and inspection requirements for the Reactor Coolant System’s high-point vents and associated components, ensuring compliance with ASME codes for safety and operability. It details how actuation valves are tested during cold shutdown conditions, outlines specific valve testing procedures, and notes that a one-time in-situ test of the DHRS function will be performed on the first installed reactor module as part of the initial test program.

edges.description: Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system components, including the steam generators, while Section B focuses specifically on the steam generators, detailing their role and design within the same system.

Section A describes the materials and components of the pressurizer, which is a part of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) detailed in Section B, where the RPV is described as containing the steam generators (SGs) whose design is to be analyzed.

Section A provides references and standards that underpin the quality assurance and material reliability requirements, which are foundational for the detailed component and subsystem design described in Section B.

Section B references Section A by indicating that the material of the RPV upper shell and upper head (components relevant to the pressurizer) is described in Section 5.2, which is listed in Section A's references.

Section A provides references and standards that underpin the design and analysis described in Section B, which details the steam generator's configuration and function, including the helical coil geometry relevant to heat transfer.

Section A describes the function and design basis of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), which relies on components such as the steam generators (SGs) detailed in Section B, establishing that the SG design in Section B is integral to achieving the heat removal performance required by Section A.

Section A describes the function and design basis of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), which relies on the steam generators (SGs) described in Section B as integral components for removing decay heat and achieving safe shutdown, thus connecting the DHRS's performance requirements to the SGs' design features such as the helical coil geometry.

Section A provides the general design basis and system-level description of the reactor coolant system, while Section B specifies the detailed components and subsystems—including the steam generators—thus elaborating on the general framework set in Section A.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system's design basis, which includes the reactor pressure vessel and integral pressurizer, while Section B gives detailed specifications about the pressurizer's materials and components, thereby specifying the materials for components described generally in Section A.

Section A provides the general design basis and system context for the reactor coolant system, while Section B specifies the detailed components within that system, including the steam generators whose geometry is to be analyzed for heat transfer adequacy.

Section A describes the materials and inspection requirements for threaded fasteners in the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B provides a general overview of the reactor pressure vessel and its internal components, including the steam generators; thus, Section A specifies material and inspection details for components generally described in Section B.

Section A provides references to industry standards and guidelines that inform the quality assurance and material reliability aspects of the reactor coolant boundary, which underpins the design and performance requirements described in Section B for the Decay Heat Removal System.

Section A provides references to industry standards and guidelines that underpin the quality assurance and material reliability requirements for the reactor coolant boundary, which are foundational to the design basis described in Section B for the reactor coolant system, including the steam generator (SG) design.

Section A lists references and standards relevant to the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary, while Section B describes the materials and standards compliance for threaded fasteners used in the reactor coolant pressure boundary, both connecting through their reliance on ASME and EPRI standards for design and material selection.

Section A describes the DHRS's function to remove decay heat and ensure safe shutdown by cooling the RCS, while Section B outlines the RCS's design as a natural circulation system, establishing that the DHRS's effectiveness (including the SG's heat transfer surface) is dependent on the RCS's configuration and operation.

Section A describes the design basis and function of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), while Section B details the material and inspection requirements for threaded fasteners in the reactor coolant pressure boundary; both sections are connected through their focus on ensuring the integrity and safety of the reactor coolant system, but they address different components and aspects.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system design, including the RCS and its reliance on natural circulation, while Section B details the material and inspection requirements for threaded fasteners used in RCS components, specifying compliance with ASME standards; both sections are connected through their focus on the design and integrity of RCS components.

Section A references inspection and testing requirements for DHRS components, while Section B provides a general description of the reactor coolant system components, including the DHRS, thus Section A specifies requirements for components described in Section B.

Section B provides a general description of the steam generators (SGs) and their integration within the reactor pressure vessel, while Section A specifies inspection and testing requirements for related components such as the DHRS, which includes steam piping connected to the SGs.
